speaker: interviewer
text: --96. And we are conducting an interview with Henry Weil. You were on the death march, and it was cold. And you saw people around you dying, because they just did not have the strength to go on. And what were you thinking about? What thoughts were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking that, you know, that I will be unable to make it. So when it got dark, around 7, 8, 9 o'clock--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how many days had you been walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days. So I tried, you know, to be the last ones, not to be in the front, and not in the middle, whatever. And two more people, you know, they [? saw, ?] you know that I-- I-- I try to disappear. They did the same thing. So slowly, you know, we run to the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: The forest was right there, you know? So we went, and we were looking for a grave. We couldn't find it. We have no tools. But we had a ditch. So we were laying in the ditch. And I was in the middle.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two of my friends-- I didn't even remember the names-- they were-- and they passed away. They died from hunger, from-- from they didn't have water, nothing, like I. But then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two men that ran away with you, that got into the ditch with you, that were on either side of you, died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Then, a week later-- a week later-- a week later, I heard [INAUDIBLE] where I was laying on the road, you know. It's, you know, it's loud, you know, The cars, trucks, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive in the forest for a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without food, I don't know. It's a-- it's a miracle, miracle without, because I was afraid even to go to look to a German, you know, for water or for something to eat. Without anything, I figured that I will be impossible to survive. So and that I was only lucky, you know, that the Americans came, you know? Because the Americans came from-- from east. From east. And-- and the [INAUDIBLE] from the camps, you know, went west. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the road. Right away they stopped, you know? Eisenhower was [INAUDIBLE] in his car. And plenty-- was plenty Jewish people, you know, in the Army. They came over, and they gave me-- right away, a doctor came that they want to see you-- are you OK-- or what I needed. They gave me a uniform to be a little warmer. They gave me to wash up. They gave me food. They asked me, where do you want to go? You OK? Where do you want to go? I would like to go to [INAUDIBLE] city. Maybe it's Jewish people coming home from the camp. So this was how you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: By General Eisenhower?

speaker: interviewee
text: By General Eisenhower. Matter of fact, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Matter of fact, you know-- you know, Eisenhower gave me two guns. And he said, you know, he took it away from the-- from the women, you know, who was watching, you know, the Jewish girls, and he give it to me. And I said, you know, whatever you do-- you know-- matter of fact-- you know, well-- and so-- and so I said, the best way, if you have a map, look, you know, for-- for the next city, because it's only forest. So the-- Hof-- H-O-F. So they all went, you know, Hof.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came to Hof. You know. They had already was a Jewish committee. Jewish already came, you know, from-- from the camp. And I was in Hof, you know, till the end, till 1946. What did you do while you were in Hof all that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was for-- I was, you know, the-- [? president ?] from the Jewish committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did you do as-- as the-- what did you do in the Jewish committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jewish committee, you know, you're trying to give, you know, people who came, you know, to get them apartments, rooms, you know, by Germans, and then food, with everything, you know? You know, I was talking-- I was busy all the time, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, in 1946, we got-- we got married. I met-- you know-- when I came, you know, already, my wife and two sisters were already in Hof. They came-- they came to Hof, and they had an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my wife's sisters, my wife's two sisters and my wife, you know? So we got together, you know? And I married my wife, you know? And the wedding was in Hof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the wedding. You know, the whole city was there. A small city, but all-- all of them, you know? All of them. Even from the other cities, you know, from around Hof, they came also, you know? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my brother? To my brother, when he was liberated in January from Brinnlitz-- he went in my place. You know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so some people from Hof went to Poland, to Krakow, to see, you know, somebody's alive. And they saw my brother. So they said to my brother, you know, your brother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he's alive. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He's in Hof. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came. He walked with me-- with me together all the time. Matter of fact, he came with his wife. With his wife. And they were in Hof, you know? And he-- when I came to the United States in 1947, and I was registered, my brother took-- took over my job. In Hof?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hof. And he was to the end, you know. But then he passed away in 1988. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They all from-- from-- from Brinnlitz, they tell me after the war here in New York, and all over, in Florida, and they said to me, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's all you can do for a brother, you give him [? for ?] Gross-Rosen, to give him your place. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm glad that I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because if he will be Gross-Rosen, he will not be-- be-- will not be alive. Because I was-- I was always stronger, all kind, you know, and-- to do things, just to make sure that, you know, I will try, you know? Maybe I will stay alive. Maybe I will make it. And I make it. So when I-- when the American-- I mean, in Hof, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when Eisenhower-- I was in-- in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE] when Eisenhower invite me-- when he was president, he invite me to-- to-- to the White House, you know? He said, you know, what-- what job your had. You know? And I told him, you know, now I'm wearing, you know, the uniform. And I'm over there in German-- in Germany, and I speak, and my name is Weil, Weil is a real German name. And maybe I will help you. I know exactly where the SS are, you know, where to-- to find the SS, the Nazis. And did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went, you know, because, you know, they were in family houses. They were hiding. I did, you know-- we did, you know? He gave me two guns. He said, keep it for a souvenir. That's just for [INAUDIBLE], you know? Until today, I still have the two guns that's just made in Italy, or whatever, you know? But it's guns, you know? And I came-- and yeah, whatever I want to do that--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hof, when it came to-- to register to-- for United States, they asked me what-- what you did before, when they made an application and everything. I said I was a gangster. I was a killer. And I knew it that-- that-- that will get the Nazis the-- and the Germans, you know? Because the Germans-- the-- the the plenty from the Americans were in the same building, you know, by [? where ?] [? the ?] registration. I knocked the door. And right away, you know, the-- the Americans came in, you know? Then-- then-- the he grabbed the German. He said, only Henry Weil will go the first one to the United States. But what he did, you know, he did very little. Because you know, one German, one from our people, they said, you know, in-- where they were liberated, they said, one German, he was good. So an-- an-- an American said, this German is good who is already six foot deep. It's not such a thing, you know? Maybe 85% or 90%, you know, even youngest-- youngsters, you know, from 13, 14 years, they gave them uniforms, you know, to put it on. They said, you will have money. You will have stores. You will have everything, you know, because so many people will disappear. That's-- they will like it, you know? Because you see, sometime, you know, the SS, you know, young-- young SS men. And they were the worst ones, because they were-- they were watching us, you know? And for nothing-- and they have a gun on the side, you know? But-- Do you remember the names of any of the Germans you-- you found?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the-- the name, you know, the name from after the war, you know, who was in, you know, in [INAUDIBLE]. You know, after we ran from the ghetto, from Krakow, the ghetto. His name was-- it was [? Goeth. ?] He was [? Goeth, ?] you know? He was very bad, for-- for so many people died through him, you know? He was very miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if he went on trial at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But he came, you know-- he just-- Oskar Schindler, he was a two face, you know? He showed to him, you know, that he's my good friend, you know, drinking together and everything. But then came after the war, and he-- he-- he told Schindler where he-- he gave him the address from-- from-- from Austria, from Vienna. When he gave him from Austria, the first thing what he did, you know, he called up the police or whatever is, you know, and he gave exactly the evidence from-- from [? Goeth. ?] And they brought him, you know, to [INAUDIBLE], to the cemetery where we were, and they were-- he-- he was hanged. You know, it was after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- was impossible. They were afraid to-- to look at him, you know? And he was right near the gate in a beautiful home, beautiful house. But in-- in-- in the camp they were built for him a house. [SIGHS] [? Goeth. ?] [? Goeth. ?] I don't know how you-- G-E-T-H, or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, you know, one more thing. When they were in the-- when-- so, you know, the Americans-- he told-- one from the Americans tell me-- told me that, you know, I-- from now on, you are free. I said, God bless America. [CRYING] That's [INAUDIBLE] I could make it, you know, because I was in maybe five, six concentration camps, I mean prisons. You know, some of them, they were death-- death camps, and some of them, they were labor camps. And I don't know how I made it, you know? When you look back on your experiences during the Holocaust, why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know why I was better, you know, to survive, and why my mother-- my mother was 48 years old when she-- when she died, you know? My father was 49 years. You could see on their picture. You can see, you know-- you know, my mother was beautiful, and smart, and always that smiling face, you know? And everybody says that sees that-- sees that picture that I'm looking exactly like my mother, you know? You know? I don't know why I was-- I was different to survive, you know, and not-- not-- not-- not mother, or my family, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. Before I go to bed, I have a big picture in the-- in the bedroom, a big one from Israel, from my uncle, my mother's brother. He went before the war. So he sent me a-- a picture. And Kodak made for me a big one, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always-- I talk to my mother. And I said, you know, goodnight. And I love you. And over here is on the wall, you know? Oh I talk-- and we come together, always from the Holocaust. You can never forget. Why did you decide to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want it-- you know-- we will not be here forever. I am 81 years old. Maybe I still have a couple of years to live, you know? Maybe I'm still strong and everything, you know? I try. Because especially my wife, you know, she went through the same thing, you know? And she was in all the papers, you know, on the wall over there, you know? She-- she-- she was lecturing. And, you know-- and-- and I don't want it, you know, the children to forget. I want them, you know, to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your most painful memory of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. My family, very much. We were always together. We were not rich people. We have the-- we had a grocery store when we came, you know, from the small town, from my father's town, you know? And we-- and we bought, you know, we rented the-- the store. And we had a grocery store, because we had in [INAUDIBLE], where my father was born. And my mother was born-- of course, you know. You wrote that down, that whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figure-- you know-- and we were always so close, you know? Matter of fact, [? as ?] I could go to Russia. I could go to Russia, you know? It'd be right in the beginning. I said, you know, the Germans, the SS are taking, you know, young people. So I was still young, and I-- and I said, no. I'm not going, because I want-- whatever happen-- will happen with them, will happen with me. I belong to-- to the ghetto. You know, because-- I-- and every single day, I-- it's in my head, you know, the memories, you know, from [? Budapest. ?] Before we end, I would just like to ask you if you have any message that you would like to leave us with? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Or message you would like to leave your children or your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: God forbid-- you know, to the children, you know, if they have to go through, you know, because they're Jewish, to go through some-- same thing, you know, what I went. Because, you know, it's impossible. You know, all over-- all over Poland, all over, you know, the forest, and in the street, and in the camps, and all over, was laying at that gas chambers and crematoriums. In the last couple hours, you know, they didn't have time, you know, to-- to put in the crematorium. When Eisenhower came to Buchenwald, he came, you know, and he saw mountains-- mountains with dead people, you know, just laying dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I said, you know, I hope they will have a better time, better time-- better life, you know, than I had. What can-- what else can I tell you? I-- I-- I wish them the best. Thank you, very, very much, Mr. Weil.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope-- I hope I told you-- I remember and I told you almost everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you did. Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: The large one, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: The large one. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, [? Yahetta Bugalia. ?] The maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Olkusz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Bianca Weil. Bianca, B-I-A-N-C-A Weil. And Henry Weil.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my brother, Wolf-- W-O-L-F, Weil, W-E-I-L, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Germany. I was visiting-- visiting in Germany where he lived, where-- where he is-- was in Hof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken eight years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law and my son-in-law, I mean, Bruce Litt. Bruce, B-R-U-C-E, Litt, L-I double T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next one is, you know, is my daughter, Joan Litt, and Bianca Weil.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the two boys, and me, Henry Weil.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of the two boys? These are your grandsons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Grandsons, Danny-- on the right side is Danny, Danny Litt. And on the left side is Brian Litt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken, you know, by the bar mitzvah, for Danny's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was in-- he is now-- he is now the 21. That was about seven years ago. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in East Brunswick.

speaker: interviewer
text: New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. 13 years, at 13--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two grandsons, Danny and Brian. Danny's on the left side then-- then Brian. And my wife, Bianca Weil, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was taken by the bar mitzvah, you know, Brian's bar mitzvah, in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me what is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, two-- two guns, you know, two rifles, or whatever, you know? That's in-- with power-- bullets, you know, that can kill you. But-- but it goes in the eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did these guns come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that comes from-- that's European.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you these guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eisenhower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.
